2024 · ACL 2024 · Agents

VisualWebArena: Evaluating Multimodal Agents on Realistic Visual Web Tasks

A 910-task benchmark that forces web agents to actually look at the page — visually grounded classifieds, shopping, and Reddit tasks where the best VLM agent (GPT-4V + Set-of-Marks) manages 16.4% success against an 88.7% human ceiling.

Jing Yu Koh, Robert Lo, Lawrence Jang, Vikram Duvvur, Ming Chong Lim, Po-Yu Huang, Graham Neubig, Shuyan Zhou, Ruslan Salakhutdinov, Daniel Fried · CMU
venue ACL 2024 citations 628 topic Agents source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By early 2024, autonomous web agents had a benchmark — WebArena (Zhou et al., 2024) — but WebArena, like almost every agent benchmark before it, was solvable by reading the page's HTML or accessibility tree. An agent never needed to look at a screenshot: every task could be resolved from text alone. That's a strange gap, because real people do not browse the web this way. Humans routinely act on a photo of a jacket, the color of a "sold" banner, or a picture attached to a message ("buy me something like what's in this photo"), none of which shows up cleanly in a DOM tree. The authors' own audit of the WebArena action space found the accessibility tree simply omits the visual signal that plenty of ordinary tasks depend on.

Vision-language models (VLMs) — GPT-4V, Gemini-Pro, and a wave of open weights like CogVLM and IDEFICS — were maturing quickly and looked like the natural fit for this gap. But nobody had systematically measured whether they actually helped on realistic, interactive, execution-graded web tasks, as opposed to static VQA or captioning benchmarks. Prior web-agent work such as Mind2Web (Deng et al., 2023) and WebShop (Yao et al., 2022) had barely touched images, and the handful of proof-of-concept multimodal web agents at the time (GPT-4V-ACT, vimGPT) were demos rather than benchmarked systems. VisualWebArena's contribution is to close that gap directly: build an environment where visual understanding is not optional — where a majority of tasks genuinely require reading an image on the page or matching an input image against page content — and then rigorously measure how far current LLM and VLM agents actually get.

// how it works

The method

VisualWebArena keeps WebArena's core formalism — the environment is a POMDP ℰ = (S, A, Ω, T) with deterministic state transitions, a fixed action space A, a partial observation space Ω, and an execution-based reward R: S × A → {0,1} — but rebuilds the task distribution and environments so that visual content actually matters. Three self-hosted, reproducible sites anchor the benchmark: Classifieds, a brand-new environment the authors built on the open-source OSClass CMS with 65,955 real listings (modeled on Craigslist/Facebook Marketplace); Shopping, WebArena's e-commerce site seeded with Amazon product data (also used in WebShop); and Reddit, WebArena's forum clone, whose 31,464 images span natural photos, memes, consumer electronics, and charts. Across the three sites the authors collected 910 tasks from 314 unique intent templates (an average of 2.9 concrete tasks per template) — six graduate co-authors wrote templates like "Find me the {attribute} {item}. It should be between {range}," which annotators then expanded into concrete, creative instructions, sometimes attaching real input images sourced from royalty-free stock photos and MS-COCO (Lin et al., 2014). A deliberate 5.1% (46 tasks) are unachievable by design, testing whether an agent can recognize a dead end and say so rather than hallucinate a completion.

Every task exposes the environment through one of four observation formats: raw DOM HTML, the accessibility tree (WebArena's default, a pruned tree meant for assistive tech), a raw RGB screenshot, and a new representation the paper introduces — Set-of-Marks (SoM), adapted from Yang et al. (2023a). A JavaScript pass labels every interactable element on the rendered page with a numbered bounding box, and the VLM is given the annotated screenshot alongside a compact text listing of each numbered element's type and content (e.g. [9] [IMG] [description: picture of a pumpkin]). The action at then just references an ID — click [31] — rather than raw pixel coordinates, which sidesteps the fact that most current VLMs were never trained to predict precise (x, y) locations. The full action space (Table 1 in the paper) covers click, hover, type, press [key_comb], tab management (new_tab/tab_focus/tab_close), navigation (goto/go_back/go_forward), scroll, and a terminal stop [answer] for information-seeking tasks.

Reward computation is where VisualWebArena earns its "visually grounded" claim: on top of WebArena's text-based primitives — exact_match, must_include, must_exclude, and an LLM-judged fuzzy_match (GPT-4-Turbo scores an output "correct" / "incorrect" / "partially correct") — the paper adds two visual primitives: eval_vqa, which asks a VQA model (BLIP-2-T5XL) a yes/no question about an image on the resulting page (e.g. "Is this a polo shirt? (yes/no)"), and eval_fuzzy_image_match, which thresholds the structural similarity index (SSIM) between a query image and a ground-truth image. Every task is annotated along two independent difficulty axes: action difficulty (easy: ≤3 actions, medium: 4–9, hard: ≥10) and visual difficulty (easy: basic color/shape/object recognition; medium: pattern recognition or OCR on short text; hard: multiple images, OCR on small/lengthy text, or fine visual detail) — a 3×3 grid the results section later leans on heavily.

R(s, a) ∈ {0, 1}  =  exact_match ∨ must_include ∨ fuzzy_match ∨ must_exclude∨ eval_vqa ∨ eval_fuzzy_image_match // six execution-based reward primitives, composed per-task by the annotator who wrote it

On top of this environment the authors build one new agent design, the SoM agent: a GPT-4V-driven policy that consumes the image-plus-caption-plus-SoM observation and is prompted with three in-context examples (one per site) using Chain-of-Thought (Wei et al., 2022). Every non-SoM VLM baseline instead reasons over an accessibility tree "augmented" with image captions — pretrained captioning models (BLIP-2-T5XL or LLaVA-7B) are run over every <img> element so even text-only LLMs get some proxy for visual content.

// what they found

The results

The human ceiling was measured directly: 7 college students (some of whom helped write tasks, but never ones they personally authored) attempted a representative sample of 230 tasks — one per template — and hit an overall 88.7% success rate (91.07% Classifieds, 87.10% Reddit, 88.39% Shopping). The residual human failures were mostly attention slips: adding an item to the cart instead of the wishlist, or giving up on an exhaustive image search after 5–10 minutes.

Against that ceiling, every model category the authors tested badly underperforms. Text-only LLM agents reasoning purely over the accessibility tree top out with GPT-4 at 7.25% overall (open-weight LLaMA-2-70B and Mixtral-8x7B never clear 1.8%). Adding pretrained image captions to the same text-only pipeline ("caption-augmented") lifts GPT-4 to 12.75% — a large jump that shows even crude, lossy visual signal (a one-line BLIP-2 caption) helps more than no visual signal at all. True multimodal agents that see the actual screenshot push further: GPT-4V with the accessibility tree plus captions plus the raw image reaches 15.05% overall, and the paper's new Set-of-Marks agent pushes GPT-4V to the paper's headline number, 16.37% overall (9.83% Classifieds, 17.14% Reddit, 19.31% Shopping) — reported in the abstract as "16.4%."

Agent (GPT-4V backbone)ClassifiedsRedditShoppingOverall
Text-only (Acc. Tree)5.56%4.76%9.23%7.25%
Caption-augmented8.55%8.57%16.74%12.75%
Multimodal (Image+Caps+Acc.Tree)8.12%12.38%19.74%15.05%
Multimodal + SoM9.83%17.14%19.31%16.37%
Human91.07%87.10%88.39%88.70%

Critically, the SoM improvement is not uniform across VLMs. The paper reports the same accessibility-tree→SoM swap for Gemini-Pro (6.04%→5.71%), CogVLM (0.33%→0.33%), and IDEFICS-80B-Instruct (0.77%→0.99%) — none of them move meaningfully. Only GPT-4V benefits, and the authors attribute this to a finding from Yang et al. (2023a) that GPT-4V is, at the time, the only model that demonstrates real SoM grounding ability, likely a function of scale or training data rather than the representation itself.

Slicing the best agent's performance by task property (Table 4) is where the visual-grounding story gets interesting: tasks requiring OCR (17.1% of the benchmark) drop GPT-4V+SoM to 13.4% versus 16.9% on non-OCR tasks — reading small or dense text from a screenshot is a real bottleneck. Tasks requiring exact image matching (8.7%) actually do better, 18.9% versus 16.2%, which the authors read as evidence that SoM's structured grounding is particularly suited to precise visual comparison. And tasks that embed an input image (25.2%) score higher too — 19.0% versus 14.9% without — the opposite of what you'd expect if images were purely adding noise; the authors note the image itself often makes the task more concrete and tractable once correctly parsed. Figure 6 in the appendix breaks GPT-4V+SoM down across the full action-difficulty × visual-difficulty grid: 25.8% on easy-action/easy-visual tasks collapses to single digits once either axis moves to "hard," and trajectory length climbs from roughly 7 steps on easy tasks to 12+ on hard ones without a compensating rise in success.

A revealing postscript: after the ACL camera-ready deadline, the authors re-ran the SoM agent with newer frontier models (Appendix B). GPT-4o reaches 19.78% overall (20.51% Classifieds, 16.67% Reddit, 20.82% Shopping), overtaking GPT-4V's 16.37%. Gemini-Pro-1.5 reaches 11.98%, nearly double Gemini-Pro's 5.71%. And a text-only, caption-augmented Llama-3-70B-Instruct hits 9.78% — dramatically better than its LLaMA-2-70B predecessor (0.66%) and closing in on GPT-4's caption-augmented result (12.75%), despite having no vision at all. The benchmark's headline gap was already narrowing within months of publication, purely from model turnover.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

VisualWebArena is a careful piece of benchmark engineering — three deterministic, self-hosted sites; six composable execution-based reward primitives; a real, sampled human baseline; and a 3×3 difficulty taxonomy the paper actually uses in its own analysis. That's a meaningfully higher bar than most agent benchmarks of its era clear. But a few things are worth reading skeptically rather than taking at face value.

The biggest one is that the "SoM helps VLM agents" finding is really a GPT-4V finding, not a VLM finding. The open-weight multimodal baselines (IDEFICS-80B-Instruct, CogVLM) floor at 0–1% regardless of representation, and Gemini-Pro actively drops slightly with SoM (6.04% → 5.71%). The paper is transparent about this and cites a plausible mechanism (only GPT-4V demonstrates real SoM grounding ability), but it means the benchmark's central methodological contribution — Set-of-Marks as a general recipe for visual web agents — is validated on a sample size of one model family. Readers citing "SoM improves multimodal web agents" should know that claim currently rests on GPT-4V alone.

The second is how fast the headline number ages. 16.37% (GPT-4V+SoM) was state of the art at ACL submission; by the time the paper was presented, the authors' own Appendix B addendum already shows GPT-4o at 19.78% — a 3.4-point jump from one model generation. Given the pace of frontier multimodal releases since January 2024 (native multimodal models, dedicated computer-use agents, and now, as of this review in mid-2026, agents built specifically for GUI grounding), it's a near-certainty that current systems clear VisualWebArena's original 88.7% human ceiling by a much smaller margin than the paper's framing suggests — or have made significant inroads. The "considerable gap" framing in the abstract needs to be read as a snapshot of January 2024's frontier, not a stable property of the task.

baselines

Fair for the moment of publication — GPT-4V and Gemini-Pro were the strongest widely accessible VLMs in January 2024, and the open-weight VLMs included (CogVLM, IDEFICS-80B) were reasonable contemporary choices, if far weaker. But the SoM technique's benefit is validated on GPT-4V only; every other VLM tested is flat or worse with SoM.

contamination

Structurally low risk at launch — all three environments are self-hosted, deterministic snapshots rather than live pages a model could have crawled. But the full task set, intent templates, and reward-function logic live in a public GitHub repo, and the paper reports no decontamination check; two-plus years and 300+ citations later, recent frontier models plausibly have seen the exact 910 tasks during training.

what's missed

The single pass/fail outcome reward conflates grounding failures (wrong SoM ID clicked), planning failures (wrong high-level strategy), perception failures (OCR misread), and execution bugs (the documented append-vs-replace text bug) into one 0. Table 4's OCR/exact-match/image-input slices are the closest the paper gets to disentangling this, but there's no per-step grounding-accuracy metric reported alongside task success, and the failure-mode writeup (Appendix C.4) is a handful of hand-picked anecdotes, not a quantified taxonomy over the full run.

would raise confidence

A systematic, sampled trajectory audit labeling failures as grounding / planning / execution / eval-noise, reported as percentages; a human-agreement check on the automatic judges themselves (GPT-4-Turbo for fuzzy_match, and especially BLIP-2-T5XL — a comparatively weak 2023-era VQA model — for eval_vqa, since a wrong VQA judgment silently corrupts reward exactly on the visually-hardest tasks); and a per-step grounding-accuracy metric reported independently of end-task success.

// bottom line

VisualWebArena is a well-built diagnostic that made visual grounding measurable for web agents at a time when almost no benchmark did — but its 16.4%-vs-88.7% headline is a moving target the paper's own appendix starts moving within months, and its outcome-only reward leaves the more useful question — is the agent failing because it can't see, or because it can't plan? — for the reader to reconstruct from anecdotes rather than something the benchmark reports directly.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. VisualWebArena extends WebArena with 910 genuinely visually-grounded tasks across three self-hosted sites (a new Classifieds environment plus Reddit and Shopping), 25.2% of which embed an input image the agent must actually interpret, evaluated with six execution-based reward primitives including a VQA judge and an SSIM-based image-match judge.
  2. Its Set-of-Marks (SoM) agent — bounding-box-and-ID annotated screenshots fed straight to a VLM, so actions reference IDs instead of raw coordinates — pushed GPT-4V from 15.05% to 16.37% overall success, but the gain is essentially GPT-4V-only: Gemini-Pro, CogVLM, and IDEFICS-80B all stay flat or drop slightly with the same representation.
  3. Even the best 2024 agent (16.37%) sat far below the 88.7% human ceiling — but the paper's own post-submission addendum (GPT-4o at 19.78%, months later) shows how fast that gap compresses as frontier models turn over, a reminder to read any single-snapshot agent benchmark number as provisional rather than durable.
